  • apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1 — exactly as requested.
  • **CEL expression** — object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).all(volume, !has(volume.hostPath)) safely handles pods with no volumes and rejects any volume where hostPath is set, regardless of its value.

### What changed and why

| Field | Before | After | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New API group for the 1.16+ CEL-based policy engine |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` | Purpose-specific kind; `MutatingPolicy` is cluster-scoped and mutation-only |
| `spec.background` | `false` | _(removed)_ | `MutatingPolicy` only runs on admission, never in background — field is implicit |
| `spec.rules[].match` + `preconditions` | JMESPath match block + `AnyIn [CREATE, UPDATE]` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules[].operations` | The CEL engine uses Kubernetes-native `matchConstraints`; the CREATE/UPDATE filter moves directly into `operations` |
| `spec.rules[].mutate.foreach` | JMESPath `foreach` over three container lists with `patchStrategicMerge` and `+(field)` defaulting syntax | `spec.mutations[].applyConfiguration` with a CEL expression | The new engine uses CEL `applyConfiguration`; `+(field)` "add-if-absent" is expressed as `!has(c.resources.requests.memory) ? "100Mi" : c.resources.requests.memory` |
| Stale version annotations | `kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.10.0-alpha.2`, `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.7.0`, `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version` | _(removed)_ | No longer accurate post-conversion |

### CEL expression details

- **`Object{...}` / `Object.spec.containers.@item{...}`** — the CEL type system for `applyConfiguration` uses compile-time-known schema types; `@item` is the item type of the named list field.
- **`!has(c.resources) || !has(c.resources.requests) || !has(c.resources.requests.memory)`** — CEL's `has()` macro checks field presence at each level, guarding against null-pointer errors.
- **`initContainers`/`ephemeralContainers`** — these optional lists are guarded with `!has(object.spec.X) ? [] : X.map(...)` so the mapper doesn't fail when the lists are absent. Returning `[]` for an absent list is semantically equivalent in Pod spec.

The converted policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every change made and why:

---

### What changed and why

| Old (`kyverno.io/v1 ClusterPolicy`) | New (`policies.kyverno.io/v1 GeneratingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: GeneratingPolicy` |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.6.0` | `policies.kyverno.io/minversion: 1.16.0` |
| Two `spec.rules[]` each with `match` + `generate` | Single `matchConstraints.resourceRules` + `variables` + one `generate` expression |
| `synchronize: true` per rule | `spec.evaluation.synchronize.enabled: true` at spec level |
| Jinja-style `{{request.object.metadata.name}}` | CEL: `object.metadata.name` via a `nsName` variable |
| Inline YAML `data:` block per rule | CEL `downstream` variable defining both resources as a typed list using `dyn()` |
| Two `generate` rules applied separately | Single `generator.Apply(variables.nsName, variables.downstream)` call deploying both resources atomically |

### Key structural notes

- **`matchConstraints`** replaces per-rule `match` blocks — the trigger (Namespace CREATE) is declared once at the spec level.
- **`variables`** holds named CEL expressions: `nsName` extracts the new namespace name from the admission object, and `downstream` defines the full ResourceQuota + LimitRange manifests as a CEL list.
- **`dyn()`** is Kyverno's CEL extension for casting values to the dynamic/unstructured type required when building arbitrary Kubernetes objects in CEL.
- **`generator.Apply(namespace, resources)`** is the built-in CEL function that creates/updates the listed resources in the target namespace.
- **`evaluation.synchronize`** keeps generated resources in sync with the policy; if the policy is updated or deleted, downstream resources follow suit — preserving the original `synchronize: true` behaviour.

Sources:
- [GeneratingPolicy | Kyverno](https://kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/generating-policy/)
- [GeneratingPolicy | Kyverno (main/dev docs)](https://main.kyverno.io/docs/policy-types/generating-policy/)

The converted policy is written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of every conversion made:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`
- Updated `minversion` annotation to `1.16.0`

**Spec structure**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `resources`, and `operations` fields
- `spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` → `spec.mutations[].patchType: ApplyConfiguration` with a CEL `applyConfiguration.expression`

**YAML anchor semantics → CEL `matchConditions`**

| Original anchor | CEL equivalent |
|---|---|
| `<(emptyDir): {}` — apply rule only if an emptyDir volume exists | `object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v, has(v.emptyDir))` |
| `<(hostPath.path): "*"` — apply rule only if a hostPath volume exists | `object.spec.?volumes.orValue([]).exists(v, has(v.hostPath))` |
| `+(annotation): "true"` — set only if annotation is absent | `!has(object.metadata.annotations) \|\| !("cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict" in object.metadata.annotations)` |

The `ApplyConfiguration` expression uses CEL object construction to produce a partial object, which Kyverno merges into the pod (only setting the one annotation key, leaving all others intact).

Here's a summary of what changed in the conversion:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` / `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `MutatingPolicy`

**`spec.rules[].match` → `spec.matchConstraints`**
- The new API uses a `matchConstraints.resourceRules` block (modelled after `MutatingAdmissionPolicy`) instead of Kyverno's own `match.any` syntax.
- Pods are in the core group (`""`), version `v1`, resource `pods`. `operations` is now explicit: `CREATE` and `UPDATE`.

**`spec.rules[].mutate.patchStrategicMerge` → `spec.mutations[]`**
- The new API uses a `mutations` list with `patchType: ApplyConfiguration` and a CEL expression instead of `patchStrategicMerge`.
- The `+(field): value` "add-if-absent" syntax is replaced by CEL optional chaining: `object.spec.?securityContext.?<field>.orValue(<default>)`. This reads the existing value if present, or falls back to the default — preserving the original "only set if not already set" semantics.

**Removed annotation**
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` was dropped since the policy now targets the `policies.kyverno.io/v1` API which is only available in Kyverno 1.16+.

Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of each conversion decision:

**API/Kind**
- `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` → `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1`
- `kind: ClusterPolicy` → `kind: MutatingPolicy`

**Match (resource selection)**
- `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Pod]` → `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups: [""]`, `apiVersions: ["v1"]`, `resources: ["pods"]`, and `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]`

**Preconditions → matchConditions (JMESPath → CEL)**
- Original: `operator: AnyNotIn` checking if `org.com/role` is absent from `tolerations[].key`
- Converted: `!has(object.spec.tolerations) || !object.spec.tolerations.exists(t, t.key == 'org.com/role')`
  - The `!has(...)` guard is needed because CEL will error on missing optional fields without it

**Mutation (patchesJson6902 → ApplyConfiguration with CEL)**
- `patchesJson6902` with `op: add, path: /spec/tolerations/-` → `patchType: ApplyConfiguration` using a CEL expression that concatenates the existing tolerations (or `[]` if absent, via `?tolerations.orValue([])`) with the new toleration entry
- `ApplyConfiguration` is preferred over `JSONPatch` here because appending to `/spec/tolerations/-` via RFC 6902 JSON Patch would fail if `tolerations` is `null`/absent — `ApplyConfiguration` handles merge semantics gracefully

**Note:** `MutatingPolicy` is namespace-scoped. The original `ClusterPolicy` applies cluster-wide. If you need cluster-wide coverage, use `ClusterMutatingPolicy` (same spec, different kind).

Here is a summary of every change made and why:

**Structural changes**

| Field | Before (ClusterPolicy) | After (ValidatingPolicy) |
|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.rules[].match` | JMESPath resource selector | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with explicit `apiGroups`, `apiVersions`, `resources` |
| `spec.rules[].preconditions` | `{{ request.operation }}` AnyIn CREATE/UPDATE | `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]` in the resource rule — no separate precondition needed |
| `spec.rules[].validate.foreach` | JMESPath `divide()` over a list | CEL `object.spec.containers.all(...)` macro |

**CEL expression logic** (mirrors the original JMESPath comments exactly)

- `!has(container.resources)` / `!has(...limits.memory)` — when no memory limit is set, the ratio is effectively 0; passes. Replaces the `|| '0'` fallback on `limits.memory`.
- `!has(...requests.memory)` — when only a limit is set, the Kubernetes API server sets `requests = limits`, so the ratio is 1.0 and always passes. Replaces the `|| '1m'` fallback on `requests.memory`.
- `quantity(...).asApproximateFloat()` — Kyverno's CEL quantity library parses `Mi`/`Gi`/`Ki` suffixes correctly, replacing the JMESPath `divide()` helper. Comparing floats is safe here since 2.5 is an inexact ratio anyway.

**Annotation updates**: removed the now-stale `kyverno.io/kyverno-version: 1.6.0` and bumped `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` to `1.16.0` to accurately reflect the new API group requirement.

**API scaffold**
| Field | Before | After | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `kyverno.io/v1` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` | New API group for 1.16+ policy types |
| `kind` | `ClusterPolicy` | `MutatingPolicy` | Dedicated kind for mutation-only policies |
| `policies.kyverno.io/minversion` annotation | `1.6.0` | _(removed)_ | Replaced by the new `apiVersion` itself |

**Match → `matchConstraints`**

The old `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources.kinds: [Deployment]` is replaced by `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules`, which requires full Kubernetes API coordinates instead of bare kind names:
- `apiGroups: [apps]`, `apiVersions: [v1]`, `resources: [deployments]`
- `operations: [CREATE, UPDATE]` — made explicit (new API requires this; the old engine defaulted to both)

**Anchor condition → `matchConditions` (CEL)**

The old `patchStrategicMerge` used Kyverno's proprietary anchor syntax `(vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject): "true"` in the patch body to make the mutation conditional. The new API uses standard CEL expressions in `spec.matchConditions`:

```yaml
expression: >-
  object.spec.template.metadata.?annotations[?'vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject'].orValue('') == 'true'
```

`?annotations` and `[?'key']` are CEL optional-type operators (available in Kubernetes 1.28+ / Kyverno 1.16+) that safely short-circuit when the field or key is absent, falling back to `''` via `.orValue('')`.

**`spec.rules[].mutate` → `spec.mutations`**

The `mutations` list item is a plain `patchStrategicMerge` without the now-redundant annotation condition block — that condition lives in `matchConditions` instead. The container, initContainer, and volume payloads are otherwise identical to the original.

raw_log:
Here's a summary of what changed and why:

**`apiVersion` / `kind`**
- `kyverno.io/v1` / `ClusterPolicy` → `policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `ValidatingPolicy`

**`spec.matchConstraints` (replaces `spec.rules[].match`)**
- The `any: [Deployment, StatefulSet]` match is expressed as a single `resourceRules` entry with both resources listed, scoped to the `apps/v1` group and `CREATE` operation. The `CREATE` operation guard that was a precondition moves here.

**`spec.matchConditions` (replaces `spec.rules[].preconditions`)**
- The replica count precondition (`>= 3`) becomes a CEL `matchCondition`. The `request.operation == CREATE` precondition is absorbed into `matchConstraints.resourceRules[].operations`.
- CEL expression uses `has(object.spec.replicas)` for safe optional-field access rather than the JMESPath `|| 'BACKGROUND'` workaround.

**`spec.context` (unchanged structure)**
- The `apiCall` block is preserved as-is. Kyverno's `ValidatingPolicy` retains support for `context` API calls with JMESPath, since label-match filtering (`label_match`) is a Kyverno JMESPath extension with no direct CEL equivalent.

**`spec.validations` (replaces `spec.rules[].validate.deny`)**
- The deny condition `pdb_count < 1` is inverted to a passing assertion `pdb_count >= 1`, which is the natural form for CEL `validations`. The `message` is preserved verbatim.

**Dropped annotations**
- `policies.kyverno.io/minversion`, `kyverno.io/kyverno-version`, and `kyverno.io/kubernetes-version` are Kyverno-internal bookkeeping annotations not meaningful in the new API group and are omitted.

      Liveness and readiness probes need to be configured to correctly manage a Pod's

      `livenessProbe` is performed by the kubelet to determine if the Pod's containers
      are running or need to be restarted. A `readinessProbe` is used by Services
      and Deployments to determine if the Pod is ready to receive network traffic.

raw_log:
Here's a summary of the key changes made in the conversion:

| Old (`ClusterPolicy`) | New (`ValidatingPolicy`) |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1` | `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind: ClusterPolicy` | `kind: ValidatingPolicy` |
| `spec.validationFailureAction: Audit` | `spec.validationActions: [Audit]` |
| `spec.rules[].match.any[].resources` + `preconditions` | `spec.matchConstraints.resourceRules` with `operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]` |
| `spec.rules[].validate.foreach` + JMESPath deny conditions | `spec.validations[].expression` with a CEL expression |
| `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers` annotation | Removed — not applicable to the CEL-based engine |

**Notable conversion decisions:**

- **JMESPath → CEL**: The `foreach`/`deny` logic (checking all three probe keys are absent) is replaced with a single CEL expression: `object.spec.containers.all(container, has(container.livenessProbe) || has(container.readinessProbe) || has(container.startupProbe))`. The `has()` macro is the idiomatic CEL way to check field presence.

- **Preconditions → `operations`**: The original precondition filtering for `CREATE`/`UPDATE` operations is moved into `matchConstraints.resourceRules[].operations`, which is the correct place in the new API.

- **`autogen-controllers` removed**: The `pod-policies.kyverno.io/autogen-controllers` annotation drives auto-generation in the old engine's webhook model. The CEL-based `ValidatingPolicy` engine doesn't support this annotation, and auto-generation for workload controllers is handled differently in 1.16+.

Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's what the policy does and why each part is structured this way:

**`matchConstraints.resourceRules`** — selects `pods` and `services` in core API group (`""`) on both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations.

**The CEL expression:**
```cel
{"foo": "bar"} + (has(object.metadata.labels) ? object.metadata.labels : {})
```
In CEL, `mapA + mapB` merges maps with **`mapB` keys taking precedence** over `mapA`. By placing the existing labels on the right side:
- If `foo` is **absent**: the `{"foo": "bar"}` default is kept.
- If `foo` is **already present**: the existing value wins (unchanged).
- If `metadata.labels` is **null/unset**: `has()` guards against a nil-map error and falls back to `{}`.

---

## Policy design notes

### API / kind
`apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` + `kind: ValidatingPolicy` — the CEL-native policy type introduced in Kyverno 1.13 and stabilised in 1.16. It mirrors the Kubernetes `ValidatingAdmissionPolicy` spec so the same field names (`matchConstraints`, `variables`, `validations`) apply.

### Resource matching (`matchConstraints.resourceRules`)
Three entries cover all requested kinds:
| apiGroup | resource |
|---|---|
| `""` (core) | `pods` |
| `apps` | `deployments` |
| `batch` | `cronjobs` |

Both `CREATE` and `UPDATE` operations are matched so an existing workload cannot be mutated to add a disallowed capability.

### Variable: `podSpec`
A single ternary chain resolves the pod spec regardless of the wrapping resource — avoiding copy-paste of the same capability check for each kind:
```
Pod        → object.spec
Deployment → object.spec.template.spec
CronJob    → object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec
```

### Variable: `allContainers`
Concatenates all three container lists (`containers`, `initContainers`, `ephemeralContainers`) into one, guarded by `has()` checks because `initContainers` and `ephemeralContainers` are optional.

### Validation expression
For every container in the merged list the expression short-circuits (passes) if:
1. The container has no `securityContext`, **or**
2. `securityContext` has no `capabilities`, **or**
3. `capabilities` has no `add` list, **or**
4. Every entry in `capabilities.add` — uppercased via `cap.upperAscii()` — is in the 13-entry baseline allow-list.

`upperAscii()` (from the `cel-go` optional string extensions, available in the Kubernetes/Kyverno CEL environment) ensures `net_bind_service` or `Net_Bind_Service` are normalised before comparison, preventing trivial case-bypass.

### `failurePolicy: Fail`
Requests are denied (not silently allowed) if the webhook is unreachable, which is the correct posture for a security control.

Written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of the design decisions:

**Three separate rules** — one per resource kind — because the path to the pod spec differs across types:
| Kind | Host namespace path |
|---|---|
| `Pod` | `spec.{hostNetwork,hostPID,hostIPC}` |
| `Deployment` | `spec.template.spec.{…}` |
| `CronJob` | `spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.{…}` |

**CEL guard pattern** — each expression uses `!has(field) || field == false`. This safely handles the common case where the field is omitted entirely (defaults to `false`), avoiding a CEL "no such key" error on optional boolean fields.

**`validationFailureAction: Enforce`** — actively blocks admission of non-compliant resources (vs. `Audit`, which would only log).

**`background: true`** — Kyverno will also scan existing resources in the background so policy reports reflect current cluster state, not only new/updated objects.

**Structure**
- `apiVersion: policies.kyverno.io/v1` / `kind: ValidatingPolicy` — the Kyverno 1.16+ CEL-based admission policy API.
- `failurePolicy: Fail` — if the webhook errors, requests are denied (safe default).
- `matchConstraints.resourceRules` — matches CREATE and UPDATE on the three resource types across their correct API groups (`""`, `apps`, `batch`).

**CEL expression**
The single `validations` expression uses the implication pattern `(kind != X || check)` — "if this is an X, the volumes check must pass" — applied once per resource type and AND-ed together:

| Resource | volumes path |
|---|---|
| Pod | `object.spec.volumes` |
| Deployment | `object.spec.template.spec.volumes` |
| CronJob | `object.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.volumes` |

Each branch:
1. Short-circuits on wrong kind, so the other two branches don't touch invalid paths.
2. Guards with `!has(object.spec…volumes)` so resources with no `volumes` field pass cleanly.
3. Uses `exists(v, has(v.hostPath))` — `has()` returns true whenever the `hostPath` key is present on the volume object, regardless of its value (empty string, null, any path), so the check is exhaustive.

**`variables.podSpec`** — a single CEL variable that normalises the three different object shapes into a common pod spec, using a ternary chain:
- `Pod` → `object.spec`

1. `has(object.metadata.labels)` — ensures the `labels` map exists at all
2. `'app.kubernetes.io/name' in object.metadata.labels` — ensures the key is present

**Policy overview**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `apiVersion` | `policies.kyverno.io/v1` |
| `kind` | `ValidatingPolicy` |
| Matches | `v1/pods` on `CREATE` and `UPDATE` |
| Validation | CEL expression via `spec.validations` |

**CEL expression logic**

The expression uses `.all()` to iterate every container in `spec.containers` and asserts all three of:
1. `has(c.resources.requests.memory)` — memory request present
2. `has(c.resources.requests.cpu)` — CPU request present
3. `has(c.resources.limits.memory)` — memory limit present

`has()` is CEL's presence-check macro, which handles the case where an intermediate field (e.g. `resources` or `requests`) is absent entirely — it won't panic with a null-dereference. CPU limits are intentionally omitted per your requirement.

If any container in the Pod is missing any of those three fields, admission is denied with the descriptive message.

|---|---|
| `object.spec.containers.all(...)` | Regular containers (always present) |

The `!has(...)` guard is necessary because CEL will error if you call `.all()` on a field that doesn't exist in the object. Regular containers are always required by the Pod spec so no guard is needed there.

Each container's `.image` is checked with `startsWith('eu.foo.io/')` OR `startsWith('bar.io/')`. Any container whose image doesn't match either prefix causes that validation to fail and the admission request is denied.

The policy has been written to `/workspace/output/converted.yaml`. Here's a summary of what it does:

**Resource matching** — applies on `CREATE`/`UPDATE` for `Pods` (core/v1), `Deployments` (apps/v1), and `CronJobs` (batch/v1).

**Variables** — three helper variables keep the CEL expressions readable:
- `podSpec` — normalises the three different resource shapes (Pod, Deployment, CronJob) into a single `podSpec` object.
- `allContainers` — flattens `containers`, `initContainers`, and `ephemeralContainers` into one list using the optional-chaining `?` operator so missing fields don't error.
- `baselineAllowList` — the 13 capabilities permitted by the [PSS Baseline profile](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security/pod-security-standards/#baseline).
- `violations` — filters to containers that add at least one disallowed capability, then maps each to a human-readable `"containerName: CAP1, CAP2"` string.

**Validation** — the single rule asserts `size(violations) == 0`; on failure the `messageExpression` reports exactly which containers and capabilities triggered the denial.
